notes.note: The Romans divided the night into four watches (vigiliae); the second watch ran roughly from mid-evening to midnight.
Praefectus fabrum: nominally the officer in charge of the army's engineers and artificers, but in practice a trusted senior aide and chief of staff to the commander; the post was Q. Lepta's.
The title acclaimed by an army to a victorious general; Cicero, hailed imperator in Cilicia, ranks the honour among the reasons he would naturally have gone out to meet Appius.
Mos maiorum, the ancestral custom that carried near-binding moral force at Rome; here, the etiquette by which a magistrate ceremonially went out to receive a distinguished colleague.

notes.anchor: of urbanity, which is a virtue, as the Stoics most rightly hold
notes.note: The Stoics classed wit and refined social grace (urbanitas, Greek eutrapelia) among the virtues; Cicero flatters Appius's polish while mocking his snobbery about birth.
Cicero coins the mock-abstractions Appietas and Lentulitas --- "Appius-hood," "Lentulus-hood" --- to deride aristocratic pride in a great family name as against true merit.
Athenodorus Cananites, the Stoic philosopher of Tarsus (later tutor of the young Octavian), cited as the authority on what genuine nobility (eugeneia) consists in.
